sentences.text: Yes.
The-- took them a few days to assemble all the, all the cattle cars.
And we were loaded, loaded on the cattle cars in Plaszow to an unknown destination.
I was-- I'm not sure.
But I was with Sam, I think, in the same cattle train.
I don't know.
I was with Bernard or Sam.
Or Bernard or my father in the, in the cattle train at the same--- in the same cattle train.
I don't Know But we were packed in.
I don't know how many people were in the train, in the cattle train.
But it was barbed-- was a little opening.
It was a barbed wire on the-- on the little window where the-- it was-- we didn't get-- I don't recall that we got any food or any water.
We were traveling.
And we don't know where was the destination.
We hardly could see the stations or anything.
People were grasping for air.
There was no air.
There was no food.
We were, for days-- I don't remember how many days in the train.
But I know a lot of people died in the, in the train.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was no water.
I, I recall I saw some people drinking their own urine.
And the heat was just terrible, terrible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the guards were sitting, sitting in the caboose.
And finally, I think we got to our destination.
And we knew we were in Mauthausen.
We were in Mauthausen.
It started the same-- raus, raus, raus.
Take, take your clothing off.
And we were going to the [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Stripped naked, stripped naked, and all we had on is our leather belt on our waist line.
Otherwise, we were completely naked.
And they were searching.
They were searching.
The kapos were searching what we have on our possession.
They were looking everywhere.
Under you arms, in your body, they were checking or you don't have anything, some jewelry or something.
And they're looking around.
I saw father and Bernard also standing in one of the lines to get into to the, to the, to the showers, delouse and whatever it is.
And I think this was the last time that I saw my father in Mauthausen, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and, and Bernard.
Well, then after the shower, we were assigned-- I don't know what happened to Father.
We were assigned to Gusen I.
Gusen I was just like a suburb, like maybe a couple miles, two, three miles, outside of Mauthausen, the main camp.
And we were assigned.
And then we worked in the granite, granite quarry every day.
We had to march to the granite quarry and break up rocks and load it into the, into the lorries.
And, and coming back, we had to march, and carrying the dead ones or the wounded ones.
Because they check how many left the place and how many went through the gate.
And this was-- the rock quarry was down below.
And above, there were towers with German guards with the machine guns.
And when they were feeling like a little excitement for them, they were shooting down.
So you never looked up.
And you never knew where you were.
Because you never-- you always were afraid to look up.
Because they were always shooting.
Well, work was very, very, very hard.
And it was in winter without gloves [INAUDIBLE].
Not in winter-- I mean, it was already [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] September, October.
And we last through the winter and just visualize.
So imagine, pick up that piece of rock, frozen rock without gloves, what it means.
But if you worked, you kept warm.
Otherwise, you freeze to death.
So you had to keep on working and slugging away the rocks.
And like I said, we had to march and sing and sing going back after work.
And then we received some soup the end of the day.
In charge of the barracks were Germans.
Most of them were murderers, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] homosexuals, and political prisoners.
There were three types of German prisoners, which everyone had a different ID.
He had-- by the collar, you could tell what he was under his number.
But mostly the Germans were in charge of, of, of-- they were the kapos.
They were the kapos here.
And it was like this.
On one of those-- I don't know-- one of those, I was sick or something.
I didn't report for work.
So I was left in the barracks.
And then the Germans had a raid, raid on the barracks.
They just came in.
They wanted a little exercise, a little excitement.
So they grabbed me.
And I was between-- there were three tiers of beds.
We slept on three tiers of bed on both sides.
And they grabbed me.
And I was between the two tiers of beds.
And they punched me.
They knocked me down.
And they were jumping with their boots on my back, try to, try to break my back.
I don't know.
I was bleeding from my mouth.
And my back was hurting.
I don't know they broke my back that time or what.
I hardly could move.
And then the injured-- the, the-- you, you take them into the washroom.
And you put them down there, because next step was always-- in Mauthausen-- was a heap the dead bodies overnight.
They were piled up in such a-- like crisscross.
The bodies were crisscross, laying 10, 12, 15 feet high, just from overnight what they died or they drown them, or whatever, whatever it was.
And then one of those, when I was thrown into the bathroom, this was already-- I was laying in the bathroom.
This was before you die.
You-- they were putting you to the bathroom down there to the latrines.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the blockalteste came in.
And he saw me.
And he performed oral sex on me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't when I-- I don't know.
I managed or what.
And I, I wash myself up afterwards.
And I came-- the following day, I went to work.
Sometimes in winter, we had to stay for hours and hours till we froze to death.
People were freezing to death.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know.
And a lot of people were reporting to the sick bed.
But who cared?
Who-- didn't care.
You never saw those people anymore.
You didn't.
After the war, we found out that they were, they were taken.
They were taken and, and gassed.
They were taken in an ambulance and gassed on the way somewhere.
They were gassed.
But we, we didn't know that.
This, we found out after the war.
Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the blockaltestes, the murderers, the whatever they were, if they wanted-- let's say there were 400 people on the-- in the barracks.
If they wanted extra rations for themself, they could kill a few people so they have more bread, or soup, or whatever for themself.
So they were doing the killing themself.
So they were doing simple killing just by drowning.
They were strong and big.
They grab the guy.
They just put his in a pail of water.
And they just drown him.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so then it looks like spring was coming.
I'm not-- don't have to go through the routine, through the details how we survived in the winters and standing, freezing, and it just-- I, I, I don't know.
It's just--
No.
You have to understand, if you are tortured and beating like this for a prolonged time, you lose, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you lose your mentality.
You're not human anymore.
You are a beast, a wild beast.
A father can kill a son.
A son can kill a father for a piece of bread.
Maybe you don't have this written in books, because you never took people and tortured them for five years and started to find that-- to come to that scientific conclusion.
So you don't have this.
But that's what it is.
We lose our intelligence.
We no more human being.
We are back in the jungle.
So that's what it is.
That's, that, that's what they wanted to accomplish.
And that's what they done.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where was I?
We're now in the spring of 1945.
Did you have any inkling that Germany was losing the war?
Did you have any information coming in?
Yes.
We started to get a little information.
Because we had, we had-- like my brother, he was very handy.
Same was very handy with the needle.
And he could sew something.
So he was making a hat or, or fix the pants for a German already.
And he was a political prisoner, a Austrian.
And, and he gave us, once in a while, a piece of bread.
He was very nice to us.
And Sam was sharing that bread with me.
And we knew-- he was telling us that is already coming closer, that is coming closer.
Because he heard some information.
But the Germans were getting Red Cross packages from home, but not us.
So for them, it was easier to survive.
There were no tortures like us.
They, they had the easy jobs.
And they were in charge of us.
And [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] so this was-- oh, so then it, it, it's already spring already.
Spring already was getting closer already.
And we-- then the, the crematoriums was working overtime.
You could smell the human flesh being burned.
It was just in the air.
And we were already very bad.
We couldn't work already because we couldn't walk already.
So they put us into-- I think they brought us into Mauthausen, I think.
And--
Yeah, I think so, yes.
And we were laying, we were laying already.
We didn't go out.
Or they didn't check on us, nothing.
Because for them was to waste-- to put us in the crematorium was a waste of energy.
Because we're going to die in a short while.
So we were just laying.
And Sam and I were laying on the, one, on the, on the top, on the third, of the third bench on top.
There were wooden benches with, with, with, I don't know, a straw on it or what.
And, and I think once a day, they used to come and throw a piece of stale bread.
Just how many hands were showing, they were throwing a stale bread.
And I don't know.
We kept a body on top to get that piece of bread.
We just showed the hand of the dead guy who was laying next to us so we got-- but we couldn't even eat that, that dead man.
Anyhow, but Sam and I were always sharing.
We were counting the, counting the, the, the crumbs, the bread crumbs, and sharing it equal.
We didn't lose our way.
And I guess we always were, were, were sharing.
Or we managed to get a little salt or something.
Once we managed to get a little salt in Mauthausen, which was a rarity to get salt, and the Russians stole it from, from under our, under our heads.
The Russians stole it.
Yes, Russian prisoners of war, yes.
They were with us in the barrack.
And, and this was already spring in 1945.
And we heard already also artillery at night and so forth.
But I couldn't get down from the, from the-- I was just laying down there, wasting away.
And one morning, it was quiet.
All of a sudden, you heard gunshots, and all of a sudden quiet.
I had a little window on top.
And I looked through that window.
And I see some tanks were approaching, tanks with a white star on it, a star.
I thought this must be the Russian, because the Russian-- I thought the star.
So anyhow, it was the Americans came in.
I couldn't get down.
I was laying.
I was bleeding internally, and my feet and everything.
I don't know how much I weigh, five, 60 pounds or what.
I was all sores and everything.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sam was in a little better, better condition than I.
He could get down.
And the Americans came.
They were starting.
First, they brought in all the Germans from the surrounding villages.
Because they always pretended that they didn't know what was going on in the surrounding.
And they put them to work.
The American dugged out with a truck, just dugged out ditches.
And the Germans had to carry the bodies and put it into--
Austrians, Austrians, Austrians-- there were-- this was in Austria, yes.
And they had to, they had to do the work.
They had to bury all the, all the dead ones.
And then the Americans came, the, the GIs, the-- and they took me from the, from, from the barracks.
And I was-- took off everything, just left the belt on me.
And I was holding on to that piece of stale bread.
They-- I wouldn't let them take it away from me.
They let me hold it, to that bread.
And they took me in American-built, somewhere nearby, a tent hospital-- clean sheets, clean everything.
They put me.
They, they washed me.
And they put me in.
And they start pumping me up with glucose.
There was was a full, full room, bit tent, with, with beds, with, with, with-- I-- they hooked me up with glucose.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was so happy that.
I-- so I ask the nurse, chocolate?
Chocolate.
[LAUGHS] She gave me the nickel, nickel.
What was it, the nickel?
What was the name of the chocolate in the brown cover, the little nickel.
It was a-- I don't know.
Anyhow, I held this in my hand.
And I fell asleep.
And it melted all in my, in my palm.
And I dreamt.
I don't know.
I dreamt I went up to heaven.
It was so warm.
It was so beautiful.
I was going up and up and up and up.
And was fire, fire all over, such a beautiful fire was.
And I felt so warm and so good.
Anyhow, I don't know what happened, or I died or what.
It's always a mystery to me.
In the morning, I woke. 80% maybe, or more, or 90% of the guys around were gone.
Everybody was dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I don't know.
I died when I came back.
Or somebody said, hey, you time is not up.
You going to stay with us for a little while to tell the story.
Anyhow, so this was the liberation.
This was their liberation.
Well, I was-- I don't know how long I was in that hospital.
It was a tent hospital.
It was American nurses and German nurses what they got.
I don't know how long it was there.
But from there, after a few, I a few weeks or I don't know, then we went to near Linz.
There was a farm, I think they said, was Hitler's farm or what-- [INAUDIBLE], a place called [INAUDIBLE].
We stayed in the farm.
We stayed in the farm, I don't know, not very long.
We stayed.
We laid on the floor in stables.
There was no room-- in stables where was horses were before.
We lay of the floor.
Anyhow, then were told that the Israeli brigade is coming to get us.
And we going to Italy.
And we going to Israel.
And then it was still Palestine.
Yes, it was Palestine.
Yes, the Jewish brigade-- they were in British uniforms.
And we somehow-- yes, they came.
They came.
And they loaded, loaded up us on, on train with Italian prisoners of war returning home.
We were smuggled in through the Alps.
And we came to a place.
By trains, by trains, yes.
Yes.
I remember looking down from the Alps for the first time and being in such a high altitude.
It was very impressive to see all the little villages below.
It was very, very, very impressive.
And we came to Italy.
I think we were staying in Modena, I think, Modena, overlooking the ocean in tents.
And there was a [INAUDIBLE] camp.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we were still very sick, very weak.
We were needing nourishment.
But there none.
There was a little soup, a piece of bread.
I saw, I saw the fellows who were with us were going out in the streets and picking up butts and smoking.
And it wasn't good.
So-- and then a couple weeks later, maybe, or so, we heard some, some guys in the British uniforms with Polish eagle on the berets walking by and talking Polish.
So we approached them.
And we started talking to them.
And they said they are from the Polish army under the British command.
And they told us a story.
We can join the army occupation force if we want to.
So we found out and all said OK.

And we got, we got to-- they gave us some-- told us a story that a-- in a day or two, there'll be a coming through a train, going in that direction, Polish guards taking German prisoners of war somewhere to-- I think it was Bologna.
I think it was Bologna.
I'm not sure it was Bologna.
Anyhow, there is an induction center.
There was an induction center.
And, and we can get inducted into the army if we want to.
So a couple days, so we joined them.
We got on the train.
And they started joking and I got-- I got a little-- they gave me a little wine to drink.
I got a sip of wine.
I don't know where I was already.
My head was spinning.
And in the meantime, the train stops somewhere.
And I have to get over to go to the bathroom.
There was a cattle train like that.
So I wanted to go to the bathrooms.
I go off.
In the meantime, the train takes off.
A train was coming from the opposite direction.
I couldn't run across.
Train took off.
Anyhow, the train took off.
My brother is on the train.
Sam is on the train.
And I am, I'm here nowhere.
I don't speak Italian.
I go over to the station master.
And he couldn't talk.
He cranks up the telephone and asked me, do I speak Tedesco, Tedesco German.
This, this I understood.
Oh, yeah.
So he put me on to somebody.
I talked to somebody on the telephone.
And he told me that there is going to come in that direction another train in a couple hours.
So I sat and waited a couple hours.
Then I got on the train.
Then we-- and finally, I got to the destination.
I met my brother.
He was, he was worried what happened to me.
He didn't know what, what happened to me.
Anyhow, then we got some money.
And I think they gave us a uniform and sometimes.
And they assign us a tent.
And we took off something.
We got a few dollars in our pocket.
You know, we took off a couple days, something.
We came back after a couple days.
I don't know.
Then was a sergeant came to our tent.
And he says, you're going to report to Lieutenant this and this on the double.
We didn't know what happened.
Maybe they checking on us that we took off for a couple, couple days or what.
We came into that captain or lieutenant.
Oh, he was Dr.
Kimmerling from Lviv.
And he said, fellows.
First, he said shalom.
So we knew we home.
He said shalom to us.
So he said, I went through your records.
He says, and you are not ready to join the army.
He says, you have to go to convalescent.
You're not ready to join the army.
And so we said OK.
So he said, I will take you to a friend of mine, assign you to a ward to be in a hospital convalescent.
And the following day, I will come and pick you up.
So first day, he picked up my brother, Sam.
And then the following day, he came and picked me up and assign us to a hospital.
OK.
Thank you.
Here, we'll pause to change the tapes.
